metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether a title with surrounding whitespace (e.g., `'  hello  '`) is stored with whitespace stripped (i.e., `strip()` mutates the stored value) or stored as-is after passing the non-empty check — and align AC3's round-trip assertions accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan says 'trim then check non-empty' but does not specify whether the trimmed value is also what gets stored (i.e., leading/trailing whitespace is silently stripped from the persisted title) or whether only the validation uses the stripped form and the original whitespace-padded string is stored. This distinction has a concrete observable consequence: AC3 mandates that the corpus 'round-trips identically', so if `' hello '` is passed and `'hello'` is stored, the identity assertion fails; if the original is stored, then `'   '` must be rejected but `' hello '` is accepted and preserved exactly. The plan's phrasing is ambiguous and the implementer must make a semantic call that affects both the code and the test corpus design.
findings.location: What section ('trim then check non-empty') and AC3 ('round-trips identically')
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'What' or AC1 bullet: 'Whitespace is stripped before the non-empty check; the trimmed value is stored (so `"  hello  "` round-trips as `"hello"`).' This matches the analogous `→`→`->` normalisation already applied in create_core and makes the corpus design unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a normalization policy decision to AC3: specify whether the system should preserve Unicode as-is (byte-identical round-trip), normalize to NFC at ingest, or reject non-NFC inputs — and co-locate this rule with the corpus definition so test implementers have a concrete oracle.
findings.finding: AC3 references 'composed vs decomposed Unicode as separate preservation cases' in the corpus, implying a normalization policy decision rule, but that policy is never defined anywhere in the plan. The oracle for what 'round-trips identically' means when the same string appears as NFC in one facade and NFD in another is absent, leaving the test implementer with an undefined assertion target.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'composed vs decomposed Unicode as separate preservation cases'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Normalization policy: titles are stored byte-identical as received (no NFC/NFD normalization applied); the composed and decomposed corpus entries are therefore expected to round-trip to different byte sequences and must each be stored and replayed exactly as submitted.' OR 'The system normalizes to NFC at ingest; the decomposed form should round-trip as its NFC equivalent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving step for AC5 (e.g., `grep -r 'whitespace' CHANGELOG.md` and existence of the back-out note in a named file) and AC6 (e.g., `grep -c 'whitespace' docs/api-reference.md` returning ≥1 and `grep -c 'whitespace' CHANGELOG.md` returning ≥1).
findings.finding: AC5 (store scan) and AC6 (docs/CHANGELOG updated) have no associated proving command — there is no specified grep, diff, or review step to produce evidence that either the scan was run or that the docs/CHANGELOG were actually modified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC list: 'Verified by: `grep -i "whitespace" CHANGELOG.md | grep -q .` and `grep -i "whitespace" docs/cli-reference.md | grep -q .` (both exit 0).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the cross-facade rejection identity schema for whitespace-title errors in AC3: specify which attributes must be identical (e.g., exit code 1, RebarError.returncode==1, ToolError chained cause, and whether error message text must match), analogous to how ConcurrencyError identity is defined in test_parity.py.
findings.finding: AC3 asserts that corpus values are 'rejected identically across all three facades' but never names or defines the shared rejection identity schema (exit code, exception type, error message pattern). The codebase has at least three distinct rejection surfaces — CLI exit code + stderr, library RebarError, MCP ToolError — and the plan does not specify which attributes must match for rejection to be 'identical'.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'or is rejected identically across all three facades'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a conditional AC to AC5: if the store scan finds any whitespace-only titles, either (a) define and execute a migration/backfill before enforcement goes live, or (b) gate the release and document the decision — so the ticket cannot close green while persisted data is left in an invalid state.
findings.finding: AC5 states 'confirm whether any stored tickets have whitespace-only titles (a one-off store scan) so no migration is silently required,' but the plan specifies no action, AC, or artifact for the case where the scan IS positive. If whitespace-only titles exist in the store, the ticket can close green while users' data is silently stranded (old stored titles would become unreachable or cause unexpected errors on retrieval/edit after the tightening lands).
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 with: 'If the scan finds any whitespace-only titles: either run a one-off backfill that trims them (or replaces them with a sentinel like "[untitled]") before deploying the validation tightening, or explicitly document a decision to reject/hard-error on those titles at runtime and communicate it as a breaking change to affected users.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback decision rule to AC5 for when the store scan finds existing whitespace-only titles: specify the required action (e.g., 'if any found: write a one-off migration event to replace with a placeholder title, document in CHANGELOG, and block release until resolved').
findings.finding: AC5 specifies 'confirm whether any stored tickets have whitespace-only titles (a one-off store scan) so no migration is silently required' but provides no fallback decision rule for the case where the scan does find whitespace-only titles. The sub-step is left with an unspecified outcome branch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: Breaking-change handling bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit policy for the case where the store scan finds existing whitespace-only titles (e.g., 'emit a warning and proceed' / 'add a one-off migration script' / 'document as known limitation with a follow-up ticket ID').
findings.finding: AC5's breaking-change bullet says 'confirm whether any stored tickets have whitespace-only titles (a one-off store scan)' but never specifies what to do if the scan finds existing whitespace-only titles — the plan defers the real design decision (migrate? warn? block?) to the executor at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the open-ended scan note with a decided policy: e.g., 'Run `rebar scan-titles --whitespace-only`; if any exist, log them to MIGRATION_NOTES.md and open a follow-up ticket — no in-place rewrite in this PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the expected error form for each facade: CLI (exit code + stderr message template), library (exception type + message text), and MCP (structured error code/field), so ACs are testable and facades behave consistently.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that whitespace-only titles must be rejected 'with a clear error' but does not define what constitutes a clear error for each facade: CLI exit code (e.g. 1 vs 2), whether the message goes to stderr vs stdout, the message text/format, or the MCP structured error shape (error code/field). Without this, different facades may surface the error inconsistently, leaving users with confusing or silent failures.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullet 1: 'rejected on create AND edit across CLI, library, and MCP, with a clear error'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under AC 1 such as: 'CLI exits with code 1 and prints `Error: title must not be empty or whitespace` to stderr; library raises `ValueError("title must not be empty or whitespace")`; MCP returns a structured error with a defined `code` field (e.g. `INVALID_TITLE`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'Run a one-off store scan for existing whitespace-only titles and document findings' and 'Update CLI help, API reference docs, and CHANGELOG' as explicit bullets in the 'What' section, and add the relevant file paths (docs, CHANGELOG) to Scope.
findings.finding: AC5 and AC6 describe concrete deliverables (store scan for existing whitespace titles, back-out note, user-facing docs update, CHANGELOG entry) that appear nowhere in the 'What' or 'Scope' sections — they are orphan acceptance criteria with no corresponding described work.
findings.location: AC lines 5 and 6 vs. 'What' and 'Scope' sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `session_log.py` (`start()` / `append()`) as an affected consumer in Scope, and verify that `summary or _DEFAULT_TITLE` correctly falls back when `summary` is whitespace-only (e.g. use `(summary or '').strip() or _DEFAULT_TITLE`).
findings.finding: `session_log.start()` in `_commands/session_log.py` calls `create_core('session_log', summary or _DEFAULT_TITLE, ...)`. If a caller passes `summary='   '` (whitespace-only), Python evaluates `'   ' or _DEFAULT_TITLE` as `'   '` (truthy), forwarding the whitespace title to `create_core`. This caller is not enumerated in Scope. After the fix, `create_core` would correctly reject it — but the caller may not handle the resulting `CommandError` gracefully, or callers of `rebar.append_session_log()` / `rebar.start_session_log()` with a whitespace `summary` may hit an unexpected error.
findings.suggested_fix: In `session_log.start()` (and `append()` where it auto-creates), replace `summary or _DEFAULT_TITLE` with `(summary or '').strip() or _DEFAULT_TITLE` so whitespace-only summaries fall back to the default title rather than propagating to `create_core` as a whitespace string.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'moderately large text' with a concrete size bound (e.g., 'a string of 200 printable ASCII characters — below the 255-char limit but large enough to stress serialization').
findings.finding: 'Moderately large text' in the cross-facade corpus (AC3) is a subjective term with no stated size, leaving the executor to guess what qualifies as sufficient coverage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; also What bullet 3
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'moderately large text: a 200-character ASCII string (deliberately below the 255-char rejection threshold to verify round-trip, not rejection).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the Scope section to list all files/modules where validation must change (library entry point, MCP surface) so it matches the cross-facade requirement in 'What' and AC1.
findings.finding: Scope names only `src/rebar/_commands/composer.py` and `tests/interfaces/facades/`, but 'What' and AC1 require whitespace-rejection enforced 'consistently across the CLI, library, and MCP create/edit surfaces' — if library and MCP have independent validation entry points, those files are absent from Scope, creating a contradiction between what must change and what is in scope.
findings.location: Scope section vs. 'What' bullet 1 and AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: the library public API entrypoint (e.g. `src/rebar/api.py` or equivalent) and the MCP tool handler file where title inputs are accepted, alongside `composer.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the vague 'back-out/expand-contract note' in AC5 with a concrete rollback/expand-contract specification: e.g., a feature flag to re-enable loose validation for one release, or an explicit git-revert + re-release procedure, or a deprecation warning phase before hard rejection.
findings.finding: AC5 requires a 'back-out/expand-contract note' for the breaking whitespace-title change, but neither AC5 nor any other section specifies the actual rollback mechanism or expand-contract steps (e.g., a deprecation window, a flag, or a concrete revert procedure) — acknowledgement of breakage alone is insufficient under T4.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider an expand-contract sequence: Phase 1 — emit a warning (not error) for whitespace-only titles for one release; Phase 2 — hard-reject. Document the revert commit/tag so ops can pin to it if needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name a specific target artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc section) for the whitespace-rejection contract decision and back-out note, not just a CHANGELOG entry.
findings.finding: The plan calls for a 'back-out/expand-contract note' and breaking-change documentation, but does not name a discoverable artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc) where the whitespace-rejection contract decision will be captured — a CHANGELOG entry alone is insufficient for a cross-facade behavioral contract.
findings.location: AC: Breaking-change handling bullet / AC: User-facing docs updated bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC bullet: 'Add an entry to CLAUDE.md (or create `docs/decisions/NNN-title-validation.md`) capturing the whitespace-rejection contract, the expand-contract rationale, and the back-out policy, cross-referenced from the CHANGELOG entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `idea` (CLI `idea_cli`, library `rebar.idea()`, MCP `create_idea`) to the enumerated consumer list in Scope, or explicitly confirm the fix is anchored in `create_core` so all callers inherit it.
findings.finding: The `rebar idea` surface — `_commands/idea.py` (CLI), `_lib_writes.idea()` (library), and `_mcp_writes.create_idea` (MCP) — all call `create_core` and will implicitly receive the whitespace-title fix, but are not enumerated as consumers in the Scope or Why sections. AC item 1 says 'across CLI, library, and MCP create/edit surfaces' without naming these entry points, leaving a gap in coverage acknowledgment: if `create_core`'s fix is applied correctly, `idea` is covered for free; but if a reviewer adds a facade-level pre-check instead of (or in addition to) fixing `create_core`, `idea` surfaces would be silently missed.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add a note: 'The fix must be applied in `create_core` (not at the facade layer) so `idea_cli` / `rebar.idea()` / `create_idea` inherit it automatically. AC corpus tests should include at least one whitespace-only title rejection case exercised through `idea` surfaces.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale to the plan explaining why the fix belongs in `create_core`/`edit_core` (e.g., 'All three facades route through these shared functions, so a single fix is inherited uniformly — consistent with the existing `validate_tag_name` pattern in `_seam.py`').
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why the chosen fix point (centralized validation inside `create_core`/`edit_core`) is preferred over alternatives. The 'Why' section motivates the feature (whitespace bug exists) but does not state why fixing the shared core layer — rather than, say, adding guards at each facade boundary — is the right architectural choice. This rationale is absent.
findings.location: Why / What / Scope sections (no rationale for the chosen fix point)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' or 'Scope' section, add: 'The fix belongs in `create_core` and `edit_core` because every facade (CLI, library, MCP) routes through them — exactly like the `validate_tag_name` guard in `_seam.py` — ensuring the contract is enforced uniformly with no per-facade duplication.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
